meta.title: Royal Palace of Phnom Penh | Visitor Guide, History & Tickets
meta.description: Plan your visit to the Royal Palace of Phnom Penh — opening hours, $10 ticket, strict dress code, the Silver Pagoda's 5,329 silver tiles, the 9,586-diamond gold Buddha and 600m of Reamker murals.

notice.title: Notice
notice.text: The palace closes for lunch (11:00–14:00) and may close temporarily during royal ceremonies. Shoulders and knees must be covered.

hero.title: Royal Palace of Phnom Penh
hero.subtitle: A living royal residence and Cambodia's most sacred cultural landmark, where Khmer tradition meets French colonial elegance on the banks of the Four Rivers.

about.title: About the Royal Palace
paragraphs: The Royal Palace of Phnom Penh was founded in 1866 by King Norodom I, who moved the capital back to Phnom Penh from Oudong. He chose this site at the confluence of the Four Rivers — the Tonlé Sap, the Upper Mekong, the Lower Mekong and the Bassac — a place Khmer geomancy regards as a protective, prosperity-bringing heart of the kingdom.

More than a museum, the palace remains the official residence of the Cambodian royal family and the stage for coronations, royal weddings and state ceremonies. Its layout blends traditional Khmer architecture with the French colonial influences introduced during the protectorate.
Did you know? When the golden royal flag flies above the palace's tallest spire, it means the reigning monarch — King Norodom Sihamoni — is inside.

timeline.title: A brief history
timeline.subtitle: From a royal vision to a living heritage

events.title: Capital moved to Phnom Penh
events.desc: King Norodom I relocates the court from Oudong and begins building the palace complex.

events.title: Throne Hall completed
events.desc: The central ceremonial hall rises with its 59-metre gilded tower.

events.title: Silver Pagoda established
events.desc: A wooden sanctuary for royal relics is built; later reclad in solid silver.

events.title: Reamker murals painted
events.desc: Artists cover the cloister walls with 600 m of the Khmer Ramayana.

events.title: Restored as royal residence
events.desc: After turbulent decades, the monarchy is reinstated and the palace reopens to the public.

architecture.title: Highlights & legends
architecture.intro: Centuries of craftsmanship, faith and royal spectacle — here are the treasures you should not miss.

items.name: Throne Hall (Preah Tineang Tevea Vinichhay)
items.nameLocal: ព្រះទីនាំងទេវវិនិច្ឆ័យ
items.desc: Topped by a 59-metre golden tower blending Khmer prasat form with Brahmin symbolism. Used for coronations, receptions of foreign heads of state and royal audiences. Photography inside is strictly forbidden.

items.name: Silver Pagoda (Wat Preah Keo Morakot)
items.nameLocal: វត្តព្រះកែវមរកត
items.desc: The floor is laid with 5,329 solid silver tiles (about 1 kg each). Inside stands a life-size 90 kg solid-gold Buddha encrusted with 9,586 diamonds — the largest a 25-carat stone at the crown — and a 17th-century crystal (Emerald) Buddha said to bring peace and harvest.

items.name: The Reamker Cloister
items.nameLocal: ចម្លាក់រាមកេរ្តិ៍
items.desc: A 600-metre gallery of murals (painted 1903–1904) narrating the Reamker, Cambodia's version of the Ramayana — battles of gods and demons, apsara dancers and the loyal monkey-god Hanuman.

items.name: Chanchhaya Pavilion (Moonlight Pavilion)
items.nameLocal: ព្រះរាជបន្ទំចន្ទឆាយា
items.desc: An elegant open-air pavilion facing the avenue, historically the royal grandstand for classical Apsara dance performances and ceremonial parades.

items.name: Royal Gardens & Napoleon III Pavilion
items.nameLocal: ឧទ្យានព្រះរាជវាំង
items.desc: Shady lawns, topiary and a small iron pavilion gifted by France — a calm place to rest between sights.

visitorGuide.title: Plan your visit
visitorGuide.hoursValue: 08:00–11:00 and 14:00–17:00. Closed for lunch (11:00–14:00). May close temporarily for royal ceremonies or state events.
visitorGuide.durationNote: Go early (08:00) or after 14:30 to beat the heat and the crowds.
tips: Hire an official guide at the entrance ($10–$15) for royal stories and hidden details.
Carry USD cash — tickets are sold on-site only.
No photography inside the Throne Hall or the Silver Pagoda.
Watch the royal flag: if it flies, the King is in residence.
route: Enter at the main gate and pause at the ticket booth.
Stroll the Royal Gardens toward the Throne Hall (view the exterior and staircase).
Visit the Silver Pagoda and the 600 m Reamker cloister.
See the royal regalia, carriages and costume exhibition.

ticket.title: Tickets
ticket.notes: Includes the palace grounds, Throne Hall exterior and the Silver Pagoda. Photos are NOT allowed inside the Throne Hall or the Silver Pagoda. Bring your passport; the dress code is enforced.

dressCode.title: Dress code (strictly enforced)
dressCode.intro: The palace is an active royal and religious site. Visitors in sleeveless tops, shorts or skirts above the knee are regularly turned away — even a scarf over the shoulders may not be accepted.

items.title: Not allowed
items.desc: Sleeveless tops, shorts, short skirts, ripped jeans, crop tops, see-through fabrics.

items.title: Required
items.desc: Shoulders and knees fully covered — a T-shirt with sleeves and long trousers or a long skirt.

items.title: Comfort
items.desc: Modest, breathable clothing and comfortable shoes; you will walk outdoors in the heat.

transport.title: Getting there
transport.intro: The palace sits on Samdach Sothearos Blvd, facing the Tonlé Sap river in central Phnom Penh.

modes.name: By air
modes.desc: Phnom Penh International Airport is about 30 min by taxi to the palace.

modes.name: By tuk-tuk / taxi
modes.desc: The easiest way across town; ask for 'Royal Palace' (preah ba-rom rea-chh veang).

modes.name: On foot
modes.desc: Walkable from the Riverside, the National Museum and Wat Phnom in 10–20 min.

steps.title: From the airport
steps.detail: Take an official taxi from the airport rank (flagfall ~$12–15 USD, 30–40 min). Show the driver 'Royal Palace, Samdach Sothearos Blvd'.

steps.title: From downtown / Riverside
steps.detail: Hail a tuk-tuk or passapp (ride-hailing app); the fare is usually $2–4 USD. Ask for 'Preah Barom Reachea Vaeng'.

steps.title: By tuk-tuk to the gate
steps.detail: Ask to be dropped at the main entrance on Samdach Sothearos Blvd, facing the river — the ticket booth is just inside.

steps.title: On foot
steps.detail: If you are along Sisowath Quay, walk north ~3 min; the palace walls and golden spires are unmistakable.

gallery.title: Photo gallery
gallery.caption: Glimpses of the gilded spires, silver floors and tranquil gardens of the Royal Palace.

nearby.title: Nearby attractions
nearby.intro: Make a day of the riverside quarter.

food.title: Food nearby
food.intro: Refuel along the river after your visit.

items.name: Khmer traditional
items.desc: Try amok, lok lak and beef salad at riverside restaurants.

items.name: Riverside cafés
items.desc: Shaded spots for iced coffee, fresh coconut and people-watching.

items.name: Night market
items.desc: Evening stalls for grilled skewers, noodles and tropical fruit.

reviews.title: What visitors say
tips: Hire a guide for the stories

photography.title: Photography tips
photography.intro: The grounds are a dream for photographers — just respect the no-flash, no-interior rules.

times.name: Morning (08:00–10:00)
times.desc: Soft light and fewer crowds; cool before the heat.

times.name: Late afternoon (15:00–17:00)
times.desc: Warm gold light on the spires; long shadows in the gardens.

bestTime.title: Best time to visit
bestTime.intro: Phnom Penh is hot year-round; plan around the heat and the rains.

seasons.note: Most comfortable; peak season, arrive early.
Very hot; visit at opening or late afternoon.
Afternoon showers; mornings are clear and green.

items.q: How much is the ticket and where do I buy it?
items.a: About $10 USD, paid in cash on-site at the entrance. Cards are generally not accepted, so bring US-dollar bills.
Strongly recommended. Official multilingual guides (including Chinese) at the entrance cost about $10–$15 and unlock royal history and hidden details.
Shoulders and knees must be fully covered. Sleeveless tops, shorts and short skirts are not allowed, and a scarf over the shoulders may be refused.
08:00–11:00 and 14:00–17:00, with a midday closure from 11:00 to 14:00. The palace may also close for royal ceremonies.
You may photograph the gardens and exteriors, but photography and video are strictly forbidden inside the Throne Hall and the Silver Pagoda.

items.q: How long should I plan for?
items.a: About 1.5–2 hours for the highlights: Throne Hall exterior, Silver Pagoda, the Reamker cloister and the royal regalia exhibition.

footer.siteName: Royal Palace of Phnom Penh Guide
footer.description: An independent visitor guide to Cambodia's Royal Palace — history, tickets, dress code and tips. Not affiliated with the palace administration.
